title: build: support building from `commit` when using `git` repo as build context
body:
When using URL to a git repo as build context allow specifying `commit`
to make sure specific `commit` is used as the build context.

Examples

```console
buildah build -t test myrepo.git#abcef123
buildah build -t test myrepo.git#abcef123
buildah build -t test myrepo.git#abcef123:somesubdir
```
Closes: https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues/4148

comments.body:
> When using URL to a git repo as build context allow specifying `commit` to make sure specific `commit` is used as the build context.
> 
> Following feature is incorporated by adding `?` to already supported syntax for `git` repo as build context.

Is `?` following some established convention? If not maybe we could
consider `@` instead. That seems a bit more intuitive to me.

```
buildah build -t test myrepo.git#@abcef123
buildah build -t test myrepo.git#somebranch@abcef123
buildah build -t test myrepo.git#somebranch:somesubdir@abcef123
```

Also, should we update docs anywhere? Or maybe it's the podman docs we should update?

> > When using URL to a git repo as build context allow specifying `commit` to make sure specific `commit` is used as the build context.
> > Following feature is incorporated by adding `?` to already supported syntax for `git` repo as build context.
> 
> Is `?` following some established convention? If not maybe we could consider `@` instead. That seems a bit more intuitive to me.
> 
> ```
> buildah build -t test myrepo.git#@abcef123
> buildah build -t test myrepo.git#somebranch@abcef123
> buildah build -t test myrepo.git#somebranch:somesubdir@abcef123
> ```
> 
> Also, should we update docs anywhere? Or maybe it's the podman docs we should update?

Sure I am fine to use `@` since `docker` does not implements this feature so I think we can choose anything which everyone agrees with.

What's the difference between 

```
buildah build -t test myrepo.git#@abcef123
```

and

```
buildah build -t test myrepo.git#somebranch@abcef123
```

Will the first one only work if the commit is on the main branch in the repo?

When `docker build` is told to use a Git repository, it apparently does something more like this:
```console
git init $tempdir
git remote add origin first-part-of-url
git fetch --depth=1 origin -- second-part-of-url-if-given-or-just-"master"
git checkout second-part-of-url-if-given-or-just-"master"
```
This apparently lets the user not care if the second part of the URL is a branch name, a tag, or a commit ID, which is simpler.  It also uses "--depth=1", so we wouldn't be breaking any end-user expectations if we started using it.

Or perhaps we can default to `HEAD`, if we're sure it's always going to be pointing to some branch or another, so we don't have to guess whether `master` or `main` is the name of the "default" branch.
I think using HEAD would be best ide.

> Or perhaps we can default to `HEAD`

Okay, `gitrepository-layout(5)` says it should be there in a valid repository, so I think it's safe to assume it will be.

The special case for locations that start with "github.com/" that starts at line 147 either needs to be change to let them be handled by the branch that clones the repository, or the new build being done in the "bud-github-context" test needs to be changed to specify the build context location with the "git://" prefix.
The check for a ".git" suffix at line 132 wasn't updated when we added subdirectory support.  We can either fix that now or later.
@nalind PTAL, new test uses a `local` git repo with `git-daemon` and verifies that `Containerfle` exists on older commit and not on `main` branch.
